# Transcrição: 11-Rapid Fire Testing - Round #1

- **Fonte:** 11-Rapid Fire Testing - Round #1
- **Duração:** 18:42
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:00]

Alright guys, so now we're coming to the stage where we're ready for the first round of our rapid fire testing. Now remember there's basically three rounds to a rapid fire test. The first round is where you're either testing out the personas if it's a brand new ad account or a brand new business. So you're trying to figure out which one of those personas are going to be the easiest ones to go after initially. If it isn't existing ad account instead of testing out personas, what you're actually doing is just testing out audiences within a persona. So you know who the persona is and you're just trying out different audiences within that persona. The second round of a rapid fire test is where we test out the offer the hooks. So is it free lay by? Is it free shipping? Is it a coupon? Is it a specific product? Is there something in our shop that works really well? And then in many ways, you know, when you're doing your first round of rapid fire testing you're kind of playing around with that already. So it gets easier and easier. The first round is always the hardest one because it's a really big stab in the dark. From there you start to have some data. So when you get to round two you actually already have a bit of an idea of what's going to work with the offers because you would have seen some traction and data from your rapid fire tests in the first round. And again in the third round this is where we crack down on your creative, we know the audience, we now know the offer in the hook, now we're trying to figure out, you know, which thumbnail image works best, which visual works best, which ad format works best to communicate this offer. And so those are the rapid fire rounds. Now we're in the first stage here and this is where we're going to be in this specific example we're going to be testing out the personas. So remember here this is basically what we're rolling it with here and we've also got our funnel tracking sheet which we've outlined here. Now I've got the ad account ready to go and I've kind of already set one or two things up, but there's a couple really important things that I need to talk you guys through here on the setup of a rapid fire test. So basically remember the sheet with your personas, right? So you've mapped out the different personas, in this case we identified new parents, we also identified grandparents as well as toddlers. So what I've done here is I've inside of the second sheet of our funnel tracking, I've basically mapped out, I've laid out the different personas and then I've also started to build out some of the targeting ideas here. Now remember in the initial draft we wanted a lookalike audience here. So that's pretty much the ideal situation is to, sorry let me go back to the sheet,

## [03:00 - 06:01]

is to set up a lookalike, so testing out an interest based audience with a lookalike. However in this scenario the client never really segmented out their lists so the lookalike audience we would be able to create a lookalike audience of people that purchased. Now what I can actually do after this round, so let's say I have a video or a slideshow inside of my rapid fire testing targeting new moms, so maybe it's the five accessories for newborn babies. I can then actually create a custom audience of people that watched 75% and up of that video and then I can create a lookalike audience from that. So I can build from it but for the moment I don't have anything to work with so I'm going to go broad targeting. And then with the actual targeting this is what I did. So I went into audience insights and I segmented out my countries which is important so I know I'm going after Australia and New Zealand. So this one is going after new moms so we've got this age. And then what I did was we basically picked child, zero to twelve months old and do you know what, like people that are engaged are probably about to have kids as well. ## [06:01 - 09:03]

you just, you know, deselect this and you write down pumpkin patch inside of your targeting in here, you go back in here and now what you're going to do is drill down even more by going pumpkin patch. It's a shame because you used to be able to pull custom audiences in here like people that purchased and it would show you this info but people were mining that data and they revoked the right to do that. So it keeps giving you more and more and more ideas so you kind of have to have a base of either a brand, a competing brand that shares the same audience or maybe it's a conference or an event or some kind of behavior or demographic to get started with and as soon as you know one of those then you can kind of go down a rabbit hole and pull up a lot of stuff. So that's what I did here, I pulled up a whole bunch of stuff. Now I have a budget of $300 for this entire rapid fire test which is very, very small for a rapid fire test, it's kind of like the minimum and already I've got one, two, three, four, five, six ad sets, right? So even though they're targeting New Zealand and Australia and as much as I would love to be able to segment those out separately, the reality is I don't really have enough of a budget. We want to allocate about $15 a day per ad set. Now you can run it at $5 a day, that's up to you but the reason we do $15 is that if you need enough impressions, you need enough data through and usually that's about a thousand impressions, one to two thousand impressions per ad set and you need enough data to really assess whether or not your persona is working or not because sometimes results can be a little bit wonky in the beginning, they can start really high or really low and then they tend to settle in after a certain amount of impressions. And so if you're spending $5 a day, it means you're basically like prolonging that testing phase, you're almost like torturing yourself. So we prefer to do $15, I wouldn't start them higher than $30 though. Now if your client gave you a one to two thousand dollar budget for your rapid fire testing or for the month of testing, what this means is that you would probably have a thousand dollars here for round one, which means you could actually split out Australia, New Zealand so you'd have at that stage eight ads, no, no, no, no, no, wait, I'm really bad. So we've already got six ad sets so you've had 12 ad sets if you split out Australia and New Zealand each one at $15 a day. But I'm going to keep these combined for now because I just don't have that kind of budget right now. So let me just remove that and we're going to do our broad targeting.

## [09:03 - 12:08]

Now one of the things here is Mother of Toddler and New Expecting Moms is very, very similar age and so with this broad targeting it makes no sense really because this really should just go into one ad set because it's pretty much going to be the exact same targeting women living in Australia and New Zealand age 25 to 39. And so what we're going to do here is we're actually going to combine one ad set and show the same creative for both personas under that ad set. So I can either remove this or I can actually test out LLA. So I'm going to test out an LLA of 1% purchased here because I do like to test out look-alikes as well. We're going to remove that. And then here with our grandparents we will do a broad targeting because it's a different age bracket altogether so it's not really overlapping with this one here. So these are the ad sets that I'm running. I'm going to have six ad sets inside of the ad account. Now this is the other thing I want to take you guys through which is quite important is let me go back inside of ads manager. Ideally you want to test out about 10 creatives per ad set. Now we know that these are different personas so I'm not going to have, I'm going to have very similar creative but the copy might be a little bit different and some of the images might be a bit different. For example for new and expecting moms well we want to show a lot of baby photos right, baby toys, the swaddles, etc. etc. because that's what's relevant to them. Whereas for mothers of toddlers the baby stuff maybe isn't as relevant so we need to show more toddler stuff and the grandparents we can show both but the copy probably needs to be different. It needs to be more around gifting right, around that, spoiling your grandchildren, the theme of that. So we want to have about 10 creatives per ad set. The creatives with these would be exactly the same except as I mentioned because we're adding our mother of toddlers because it's pretty much exactly the same broad audience. We're going to pull the creative from this one into this one as well. So here we'll have a lot more creative to test with. Now if you are running a tiny, tiny budget like this I don't necessarily think 10 is necessary because it's going to be hard for you to really get some traction on those ads so I would almost split that in half. I would almost have five creatives. So remember in the creative training that I take you guys through we have an approach on how we test out creatives in our rapid fire test so we like to test out a video, a collection ad format, a carousel, and a single image and a single image headline ad

## [12:08 - 15:10]

as well. So you can basically take that approach and test out the five different ad formats with copy relevant to your persona. Now when you're setting up your first round of rapid fire testing what I've done here is I've set up this campaign here. This is just launched as you can see it's only spent 20 cents here. And what I did was I literally went in here because we've already mapped out our naming conventions remember that and I've basically copied this and then this is what I use for my naming convention. So across my funnel maps, my lucid chart, everything it's consistent. People know what I'm doing. Now I've set this up as a post engagement objective campaign and not a conversion objective. Now you can set it up as a conversion objective campaign. However, what I would say to that is just be careful. The reason I like post engagement especially on brand new accounts or accounts that haven't done too well with Facebook ads in the past is that remember what I was telling you guys about the algorithm. When you run a conversion objective campaign part of the algorithm considers the conversion rate so the expected action part of the algorithm that goes in the auction it takes into account how well things are converting for you. And Facebook doesn't like it if it's less than 8%. That's what it says right. I've seen a lot of campaigns that do really really well even when it converts less than that. However, in the rapid fire test we kind of just want to eliminate that as a variable altogether. We don't even want that to be an issue. And so running it as a post engagement takes that variable out and while we're not necessarily looking for things like purchases or add to cart what we want to look at is the soft stats, the responsiveness of our audience. Because we know we're running this to good traffic placements like newsfeed and Instagram. So try to avoid your audience network at the top of the funnel during a rapid fire test. Keep it to newsfeed and Instagram. Now the second thing that I want to talk to you about here is you may be asking well okay that's great you're running a post engagement objective campaign great but how are you getting carousel ads and single image headline ads inside of that campaign objective because you can't create those kinds of ad formats inside a post objective. So this is what this is here. This campaign here top of funnel test ads is what I call a dud campaign. It's never going to go live. It's never going to get any ad spend towards it. In fact there's no targeting inside of here. The whole thing is just set up purely so that I can build those kinds of ads.
